paragraphs.body: My secret language is the language I speak with ------------ ┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡┬¡my family. It is a language of Jewish families. People who are not Jewish cannot understand it. My language has grown and evolved with me and my family. It is unique and meaningful to me because it helped shape me into the person I am today. Although this language is spoken amongst my entire family, it is more specifically spoken amongst one particular subgroup in my family.

paragraphs.body: I grew up in a reformed Jewish household where nothing was ever that serious. I personally am not religious at all, but Jewish slang is still something I grew up around. Schmatta is a common word frequently said amongst my family, more specifically my grandma. A schmatta is just any kind of towel, cloth, or covering. Another frequent one used in my house is the word ÔÇ£kvetch. To ÔÇ£kvetchÔÇØ just means to complain. For instance, every time I had homework to complain about, my mom would tell me to ÔÇÿÔÇ£stop kvetchingÔÇØ and just do my work.ÔÇÖ Another common word I hear a lot when IÔÇÖm at home is ÔÇ£schlep.ÔÇØ To schlep means to drag something around, but in my house, it was used to describe feeling sick. If me or my brother wasnÔÇÖt feeling good, she would ask ÔÇ£are you sick-sick or just schleppy?ÔÇØ I am unsure if that's how other Jewish families use ÔÇ£schlepÔÇØ or if itÔÇÖs just mine. I remember saying the word ÔÇ£kvetchÔÇØ in front of my best friend in sophomore year of high school and she had no idea what I was trying to say. I explained it, but she didnÔÇÖt fully register it which shows this language is secret to my family only. My personal favorite word, that I even got my hometown friends to start saying, is ÔÇ£schmutz.ÔÇØ To say there is schmutz on something just means the object is dirty. My grandma uses the word to talk about if someone is swearing or speaking inappropriately. It always makes me laugh when I hear her use the word ÔÇ£schmutzÔÇØ because in any interpretation of my grandma, that word is getting used without a doubt due to how frequently she says it.

paragraphs.body: "Kiss on the keppie" is a Jewish saying I heard often growing up and still to this day. My mom used to say this to me before bed every night when I was younger. She would give me a hug and kiss on my head and then tell me to turn my lights off and go to sleep. I never realized this wasn't a common phrase until my friends asked what that meant one night when they heard my mom say it over facetime and I explained it's just a hug and kiss goodnight. Even if we were in an argument, my mom would never send me to bed without giving me a ÔÇ£kiss on the keppie.ÔÇØ This taught me to never leave an argument or uncomfortable conversation without letting whoever I am speaking to know that I care. IÔÇÖve never heard anyone else say this phrase in my life, but I have also never heard anyone in my family other than my mom say it and therefore it feels more personal and directed from her. Now, she says this when she goes to bed because she goes to bed earlier than I do. This quote is just something I grew up with and will probably end up saying to my own children one day.

annotations.text: This taught me to never leave an argument or uncomfortable conversation without letting whoever I am speaking to know that I care.
annotations.comment: Aha! What an interesting moment. This reveals a lot of heat and energy that could be the center of a whole essay!

paragraphs.body: I know I said this language is spoken within my family, but that's not entirely true. This language is understood within my entire family but is only mainly spoken within the women in my family. My mom and grandma are the ones who frequently speak this Jewish language. Being a woman in my family, I naturally say some of these words once in a while. My mom and grandma have obviously had a great influence on me not only in my language, but also in my behavior, personality, and character. They are both strong, independent, and beyond smart while also being funny, understanding, and loving. I feel like I also act in a comparable way as they do because they have had so much influence on me. Another thing that has very clearly been passed down through the generations is the ability to talk for hours on end. When itÔÇÖs time to leave a family gathering it takes about two hours to even make our way towards the door. When I was younger, this would always bother me. Now that I am older and included in the conversation, I do the exact same thing, and it drives my dad and brother insane. In these endless conversations, I know IÔÇÖm destined to hear at the very least one of the specific, Jewish words such as: Schmatta, Kvetch, Schlep, or Schmutz.

annotations.text: This language is understood within my entire family but is only mainly spoken within the women in my family.
annotations.comment: Fascinating! Do you think this idea might be related, in some way, to the one I highlighted in green just above?

paragraphs.body: The language I speak, shaped by my family, influences not only my character, but my relationships with my family and my interactions with the rest of the world. My identity has been shaped by my personal language in my family and in other contexts including my daily life, work, and school. Every time I return home, I feel proud and like I belong since the language I speak at home symbolizes my cultural background, values, and customs. My unique way of speaking has made me more adaptive and given me a better grasp of communication by assisting me in navigating a variety of social situations even if I donÔÇÖt speak my language outside of my house. The impact that my family and language have on how I see the world has also enabled me to build a solid bond with my past while empowering me to confidently confront the future. ItÔÇÖs obvious that my personal language has had profound influence on me, but more specifically the people whom I speak that language with.

annotations.text: The language I speak, shaped by my family, influences not only my character, but my relationships with my family and my interactions with the rest of the world.
